scenario: Maple Health, an insurer adjudicating 7200 claims per day, accidentally sets a DMN decision id attribute to claim status (with a space). Deployment passes, but a downstream eligibility decision that lists this decision as required cannot access its result at runtime in Camunda 8.8.
question: Why can a dependent decision no longer access the result when the source decision ID contains a whitespace character?

options.text: Decision IDs must be alphanumeric or underscore, so an ID with whitespace cannot be referenced from a dependent decision and the result becomes inaccessible
Decision IDs with whitespace are silently stripped at deployment, so the dependent decision resolves to a different ID than the one written in the source
Decision IDs with whitespace are valid only when quoted in FEEL, so the dependent decision must wrap the reference in single quotes to read the result
Decision IDs with whitespace are valid only inside decision tables, so the dependent decision must use a decision table to read the upstream literal result

a.text: Correct. The decision ID may contain only alphanumeric characters and the underscore; if it contains any special character or symbol such as whitespace, the result cannot be accessed in a dependent decision.

b.text: Incorrect. The engine does not silently strip whitespace from decision IDs; an invalid ID simply prevents dependent-decision access to the result.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no FEEL escaping path that rehabilitates a decision ID containing special characters; such IDs cannot be referenced from dependent decisions at all.

d.text: Incorrect. The form of the dependent decision is irrelevant; an invalid decision ID blocks access from any dependent decision, table or literal expression.

explanation: Decision IDs must be alphanumeric or underscore. Any special character or symbol (including whitespace) makes the result inaccessible from a dependent decision.
